result.markdown:
---
title: "Regression Discontinuity Designs"
author: "Will Horne"
format: revealjs
editor: visual
code-fold: true
execute:
  fig-align: center
---

# First: One Final DiD Walkthrough

## Did Medicaid Expansion Save Lives? {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Before we pivot to RDD, one last DiD walkthrough: this time on a live policy question
-   **The paper:** Miller, Johnson, and Wherry (2021), "Medicaid and Mortality: New Evidence from Linked Survey and Administrative Data," *Quarterly Journal of Economics*
-   **The question:** Did the ACA's Medicaid expansion reduce mortality among near Medicare-eligible adults?
-   **Why it matters:** One of the most consequential policy changes in decades + a case where credible DiD evidence has shaped the policy debate
:::

## The DiD Setup {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   The ACA let states expand Medicaid starting **January 2014**
    -   About 24 states expanded in 2014
    -   Some expanded later (Louisiana 2016, Virginia 2019)
    -   A dozen still have never expanded
-   **Treatment group:** Near Medicare-eligible adults (ages 55-64) in expansion states
-   **Control group:** Near Medicare-eligible adults in non-expansion states
-   **Before:** 2010-2013 (pre-expansion)
-   **After:** 2014 onward
-   Same 2×2 structure as the California organ donation example, just with states and years instead of hospitals and quarters
:::

## What the Data Look Like {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(30317)

n_exp  <- 20   # expansion states
n_ctrl <- 20   # non-expansion states
years  <- 2010:2017

# State-specific intercepts — centered within each group so the two
# groups have the same average baseline (keeps pre-trends visually parallel)
fe_exp   <- rnorm(n_exp,  0, 40); fe_exp   <- fe_exp   - mean(fe_exp)
fe_ctrl  <- rnorm(n_ctrl, 0, 40); fe_ctrl  <- fe_ctrl  - mean(fe_ctrl)
state_fe <- c(fe_exp, fe_ctrl)

df_med <- expand_grid(
  state_id = 1:(n_exp + n_ctrl),
  year     = years
) %>%
  mutate(
    expansion = state_id <= n_exp,
    post      = year >= 2014,
    trend     = 1400 - 12 * (year - 2010),     # overall downward trend
    fe        = state_fe[state_id],
    tx        = ifelse(expansion & post, -130, 0),  # ~130 per 100k reduction
    mortality = fe + trend + tx + rnorm(n(), 0, 25)
  )

# Collapse to group-year averages for plotting
df_plot <- df_med %>%
  group_by(year, expansion) %>%
  summarize(mortality = mean(mortality), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(group = ifelse(expansion, "Expansion States", "Non-Expansion States"))

ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = year, y = mortality,
                    color = group, linetype = group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 2014, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  annotate("text", x = 2014.15, y = 1420, hjust = 0, size = 3.5,
           color = "gray30", label = "ACA expansion begins") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Expansion States"     = "#e31a1c",
                                "Non-Expansion States" = "#1f78b4")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2010:2017) +
  labs(title = "Mortality for Adults Ages 55-64, by Expansion Status",
       subtitle = "Restricted to Medicaid-eligible adults (income below 138% FPL)",
       x = "Year", y = "Mortality (per 100,000)",
       color = "", linetype = "") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-1-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


Parallel before 2014, divergence after — the visual signature of a credible DiD.

## What Makes This Credible? {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   **Parallel pre-trends:** The two groups track each other closely before 2014, necessary (but not sufficient) evidence the comparison is valid
-   **No anticipation:** The 2012 Supreme Court ruling made expansion an unexpected state-by-state choice. Hard to plan around in advance
-   **Staggered adoption:** Later expanders (Louisiana 2016, Virginia 2019) complicate TWFE. The paper uses modern estimators (Callaway-Sant'Anna) to avoid the forbidden comparisons we discussed
-   **LATE interpretation:** The estimate applies to Medicaid-eligible near-Medicare-eligible adults in expansion states: not the whole population
-   **Bottom line:** The paper estimates Medicaid expansion reduced annual mortality by \~9% in this group
:::

. . .

::: callout-tip
## The Bridge to RDD

DiD works when you can find **two groups** — one treated, one not — and argue they would have trended in parallel. But what if there's no clean comparison group? What if the only thing separating treated from untreated is a *rule*, and either side of that rule looks nearly identical? That's where **Regression Discontinuity** comes in.
:::

# Introducing RDD

## The Setup

::: incremental
-   Sometimes, the way a policy is implemented creates a **sharp cutoff** between who gets treated and who doesn't

    -   You qualify for Medicaid if your income is below 138% of the poverty line, but not at 139%

    -   You get Title I school funding if your poverty rate is above a threshold, but not one percentage point below

-   If being just above or just below the cutoff is **as good as random**, we can estimate the treatment effect by comparing the two sides
:::

## Why Should MPAs Care? {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Government programs are **full of arbitrary cutoffs**: income thresholds, age limits, test score requirements, geographic boundaries

-   RDD lets you turn these administrative rules into **natural experiments**

-   As a policy analyst, recognizing these cutoffs, hiding in eligibility rules everywhere, unlocks powerful research designs

-   RDD is widely considered one of the **most credible** quasi-experimental designs when applicable
:::

## The Core Intuition {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Imagine a scholarship for students scoring above 80 on an exam

-   A student who scored 81 got the scholarship. A student who scored 79 didn't.

-   Are these two students fundamentally different? Probably not - the difference is essentially **luck**

-   By comparing outcomes for students *just above* and *just below* 80, we approximate a **local randomized experiment**
:::

. . .

::: callout-note
## In English

RDD exploits the fact that people right at the cutoff are essentially identical - the only thing that differs is whether they got the treatment. That's almost as good as random assignment.
:::

## Today's Roadmap {.smaller}

::: incremental
1.  **Terminology & assumptions**: running variables, cutoffs, and what can go wrong
2.  **The graphical recipe**: seeing the discontinuity step by step
3.  **Bandwidth & estimation**: how to estimate the jump and choose a window
4.  **Policy examples**: shingles vaccines and Medicare at 65
5.  **Sharp vs. fuzzy RDD**: what if the cutoff isn't perfectly enforced? (Maimonides' Rule)
6.  **Validity checks**: manipulation tests, placebo cutoffs, donut hole tests
7.  **Practical advice** + broader lessons about quant work as an MPA
:::

# Part 1: Terminology & Assumptions

## Key Terminology {.smaller}

Let's ground the core terms in one real policy: **Pell Grants** - federal need-based aid for college students. Eligibility depends on **Expected Family Contribution (EFC)**. If your EFC is below a threshold, you qualify.

::: incremental
-   **Running Variable (aka forcing variable):** The variable that determines treatment
    -   *Pell example:* Expected Family Contribution (EFC)
    -   *In general:* test scores, income, age, birth date, distance from a border
-   **Cutoff (aka threshold):** The value where treatment "turns on"
    -   *Pell example:* the max EFC for eligibility (historically around \$5,800)
    -   *In general:* any administrative line — 138% FPL, score of 80, age 65
-   **Bandwidth:** How close to the cutoff do we look?
    -   *Pell example:* students within \$500 of the cutoff? \$1,000? \$2,000?
    -   Narrower = more credible (more similar students), less precise (fewer observations)
    -   Wider = less credible, more precise
:::

## The First Picture: Seeing the Jump {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(tidyverse)
set.seed(30317)

n      <- 500
cutoff <- 0.5
x      <- runif(n, 0, 1)
y      <- 2 + 5 * x + 2 * (x >= cutoff) + rnorm(n, 0, 0.3)
df     <- tibble(x, y)

# Fit a line on each side and predict the value right at the cutoff —
# the gap between the two is the treatment effect we'll draw.
left_fit  <- lm(y ~ x, data = filter(df, x <  cutoff))
right_fit <- lm(y ~ x, data = filter(df, x >= cutoff))
y_left    <- predict(left_fit,  newdata = tibble(x = cutoff))
y_right   <- predict(right_fit, newdata = tibble(x = cutoff))

ggplot(df, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, size = 1.5, color = "gray40") +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(df, x <  cutoff), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "black", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(df, x >= cutoff), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "black", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cutoff, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("segment", x = cutoff, xend = cutoff,
           y = y_left, yend = y_right,
           color = "#6a3d9a", linewidth = 2) +
  annotate("point", x = cutoff, y = y_left,  color = "#6a3d9a", size = 3) +
  annotate("point", x = cutoff, y = y_right, color = "#6a3d9a", size = 3) +
  annotate("label", x = cutoff + 0.04, y = (y_left + y_right) / 2,
           label = "The jump\n= treatment effect",
           color = "#6a3d9a", hjust = 0, size = 4, label.size = 0) +
  labs(title = "Regression Discontinuity: Graphical Intuition",
       x = "Running Variable (X)", y = "Outcome (Y)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


The **jump** at the cutoff is the treatment effect. Everything else is smooth.

## Key Assumptions {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   **No manipulation:** Subjects cannot game the running variable to choose their treatment status

    -   If students knew the scholarship cutoff was 80, could they artificially score just above it?

    -   Age-based cutoffs (like Medicare at 65) are harder to manipulate — you can't fake your birthday

-   **Continuity:** Everything *other* than treatment changes smoothly through the cutoff

    -   No other policies kick in at the same threshold

    -   *Violation example:* Studying Medicaid's effect at 138% FPL gets complicated because SNAP, CHIP, and housing assistance can change eligibility around the same income range.

-   **No anticipation:** People don't change behavior in advance of crossing the cutoff
:::

. . .

::: callout-important
## The Big Question

Ask yourself: "Is there any reason someone *just below* the cutoff would be systematically different from someone *just above* — other than the treatment?" If no, RDD is credible.
:::

## What Does RDD Estimate? {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   RDD gives us a **Local Average Treatment Effect (LATE)**

-   It tells us the effect of treatment **for people right at the cutoff**, not for everyone

    -   The effect of Medicare for 65-year-olds, not 40-year-olds

    -   The effect of the scholarship for students scoring around 80, not students scoring 95

-   This is both a **strength** (very credible for that group) and a **limitation** (may not generalize)
:::

. . .

::: callout-note
## In English

RDD gives you a very clean answer to a very specific question: "What is the effect of treatment for people right at the boundary?" Whether that answer applies more broadly is a separate question.
:::

# Part 2: The Graphical Recipe

We'll illustrate the four-step recipe with a **merit-based college scholarship**: students scoring **above 80** on a standardized test receive a scholarship; we want to know if it raises college completion rates.

## Step 1: Plot the Raw Data {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
set.seed(30317)
n      <- 500
cutoff <- 80
score  <- runif(n, 60, 100)
completion <- 0.30 + 0.005 * (score - 60) +
              0.10  * (score >= cutoff) + rnorm(n, 0, 0.04)

df <- tibble(score, completion,
             group = factor(ifelse(score < cutoff, "No Scholarship", "Scholarship"),
                            levels = c("No Scholarship", "Scholarship")))

ggplot(df, aes(x = score, y = completion, color = group)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cutoff, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "gray40", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("No Scholarship" = "#1f78b4",
                                "Scholarship"    = "#e31a1c")) +
  labs(title = "(a) Raw Data: Merit Scholarship at Score 80",
       x = "Test Score", y = "College Completion Rate", color = "") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Step 2: Fit a Line on Each Side {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(df, aes(x = score, y = completion, color = group)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(df, group == "No Scholarship"),
              method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1, color = "black") +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(df, group == "Scholarship"),
              method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1, color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cutoff, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("No Scholarship" = "#1f78b4",
                                "Scholarship"    = "#e31a1c")) +
  labs(title = "(b) Fit a Line on Each Side",
       x = "Test Score", y = "College Completion Rate", color = "") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


We fit a simple straight line on each side. You can also use LOESS or polynomials — but linear is honest, easy to interpret, and the modern `rdrobust` default.

## Step 3: Choose a Bandwidth {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
bw  <- 4   # 4-point window around the cutoff
df2 <- df %>% mutate(in_bw = abs(score - cutoff) <= bw)

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = filter(df2, !in_bw),
             aes(score, completion), color = "gray80") +
  geom_point(data = filter(df2, in_bw),
             aes(score, completion, color = group), size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(df2, in_bw, score <  cutoff),
              aes(score, completion), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(df2, in_bw, score >= cutoff),
              aes(score, completion), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(cutoff - bw, cutoff + bw),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cutoff, linetype = "solid", color = "gray40") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("No Scholarship" = "#1f78b4",
                                "Scholarship"    = "#e31a1c")) +
  labs(title = "(c) Pick a Bandwidth: Look Only Near the Cutoff",
       x = "Test Score", y = "College Completion Rate", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


We restrict to students scoring within 4 points of the cutoff — these students are most comparable.

## Step 4: Estimate the Jump {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
df3 <- df %>% filter(abs(score - cutoff) <= bw)

# Fit a line on each side within the bandwidth, then predict at the cutoff
left_fit  <- lm(completion ~ score, data = filter(df3, score <  cutoff))
right_fit <- lm(completion ~ score, data = filter(df3, score >= cutoff))
y_left    <- predict(left_fit,  newdata = tibble(score = cutoff))
y_right   <- predict(right_fit, newdata = tibble(score = cutoff))
jump      <- y_right - y_left

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = df,  aes(score, completion), color = "gray90") +
  geom_point(data = df3, aes(score, completion, color = group), size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(df3, score <  cutoff),
              aes(score, completion), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(df3, score >= cutoff),
              aes(score, completion), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = cutoff, xend = cutoff,
                   y = y_left, yend = y_right),
               color = "#6a3d9a", linewidth = 2) +
  annotate("point", x = cutoff, y = y_left,  color = "#6a3d9a", size = 3) +
  annotate("point", x = cutoff, y = y_right, color = "#6a3d9a", size = 3) +
  annotate("label", x = cutoff + 0.5, y = (y_left + y_right) / 2,
           label = sprintf("Estimated effect:\n+%.1f pp", jump * 100),
           color = "#6a3d9a", hjust = 0, size = 4, label.size = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cutoff, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("No Scholarship" = "#1f78b4",
                                "Scholarship"    = "#e31a1c")) +
  labs(title = "(d) Estimate the Jump at the Cutoff",
       x = "Test Score", y = "College Completion Rate", color = "") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


The purple segment is the **estimated treatment effect** — the gap between each line's predicted value *at the cutoff*.

# Part 3: Bandwidth & Estimation

## The Bandwidth Tradeoff {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
bw_values <- c(1, 2, 4, 10)    # bandwidths in test-score points

df_facet <- df %>%
  crossing(bw = bw_values) %>%
  mutate(in_bw = abs(score - cutoff) <= bw)

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = filter(df_facet, !in_bw),
             aes(score, completion), color = "gray80") +
  geom_point(data = filter(df_facet, in_bw),
             aes(score, completion, color = group), size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(df_facet, in_bw & score <  cutoff),
              aes(score, completion), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(df_facet, in_bw & score >= cutoff),
              aes(score, completion), method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              color = "black", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(data = tibble(bw = bw_values),
             aes(xintercept = cutoff - bw), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_vline(data = tibble(bw = bw_values),
             aes(xintercept = cutoff + bw), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cutoff, linetype = "solid", color = "gray40") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("No Scholarship" = "#1f78b4",
                                "Scholarship"    = "#e31a1c")) +
  labs(title = "Bandwidth Choice (in Test-Score Points)",
       x = "Test Score", y = "College Completion Rate", color = NULL) +
  facet_wrap(~ bw, ncol = 2, labeller = label_both) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


Smaller bandwidth → more credible, less power. `rdrobust` can choose the optimal bandwidth for you.

## The Simplest RDD Regression {.smaller}

We can estimate the jump with OLS:

$$Y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}(\text{Running} - c) + \beta_{2}\text{Treated} + \beta_{3}(\text{Running} - c) \times \text{Treated} + \epsilon$$

::: callout-note
## In English

This gives us **two separate lines** — one on each side of the cutoff. $\beta_2$ is the **jump** at the cutoff: the estimated treatment effect. We center the running variable at the cutoff so the intercept difference has a clean interpretation.
:::

## Seeing the Two Lines {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Apply the formula above to the scholarship data: center the running
# variable at the cutoff, then fit the interaction regression.
df_scholar <- df %>%
  mutate(score_c = score - cutoff,
         side    = factor(score >= cutoff,
                          levels = c(FALSE, TRUE),
                          labels = c("Below cutoff", "At/above cutoff")),
         treated = as.integer(score >= cutoff))

rdd_model <- lm(completion ~ score_c * treated, data = df_scholar)
round(coef(rdd_model), 3)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
    (Intercept)         score_c         treated score_c:treated 
          0.398           0.005           0.096           0.001 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(df_scholar, aes(x = score_c, y = completion, color = side)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, size = 1.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Below cutoff"     = "#1f78b4",
                                "At/above cutoff"  = "#e31a1c")) +
  labs(title = "Linear RDD on the Scholarship Data",
       x = "Test Score (centered at 80)",
       y = "College Completion Rate", color = "Side of Cutoff") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


$\beta_0$ = intercept (completion just below cutoff). $\beta_1$ = slope on the left. $\beta_2$ = **the jump** — our treatment effect. $\beta_3$ = how the slope *changes* when we cross the cutoff.

## Using `rdrobust`: The Modern Approach {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# install.packages("rdrobust")
library(rdrobust)

# rdrobust does everything for you:
# - Chooses the optimal bandwidth
# - Fits local polynomial regressions
# - Provides bias-corrected confidence intervals
rd_result <- rdrobust(y = df$completion, x = df$score, c = 80)
summary(rd_result)
```
:::


. . .

::: callout-tip
## What `rdrobust` Handles

Instead of manually choosing bandwidths and functional forms, `rdrobust` uses data-driven methods (Calonico, Cattaneo, and Titiunik, 2014) to select the optimal bandwidth and provide robust inference. This is now the **standard tool** for applied RDD work.
:::

## How `rdrobust` Chooses the Bandwidth {.smaller}

The tool picks a bandwidth by balancing **two concerns that pull in opposite directions**:

::: incremental
-   **Too wide a window** → you include observations far from the cutoff, which may not be comparable. Your estimate gets *biased*
-   **Too narrow a window** → you don't have enough observations. Your estimate gets *noisy*
:::

The "best" bandwidth minimizes a single quantity called **mean squared error (MSE)** — which combines bias and noise into one number. Think of it as finding the right **zoom level**: close enough to the cutoff that comparisons are credible, far enough out that you have enough data to see the jump.

. . .

::: callout-tip
## You're Not Locked In

The default bandwidth is a sensible *starting point*, not a final answer. Always run the **sensitivity check** (Part 6) — if the estimate is stable across a range of bandwidths, the result is robust. If it swings wildly, the data is telling you something.
:::

## `rdrobust` in Action {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Let's use our simulated data
library(rdrobust)

rd_fit <- rdrobust(y = df$completion, x = df$score, c = cutoff)
summary(rd_fit)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Sharp RD estimates using local polynomial regression.

Number of Obs.                  500
BW type                       mserd
Kernel                   Triangular
VCE method                       NN

Number of Obs.                  270          230
Eff. Number of Obs.              76           73
Order est. (p)                    1            1
Order bias  (q)                   2            2
BW est. (h)                   5.197        5.197
BW bias (b)                   7.636        7.636
rho (h/b)                     0.681        0.681
Unique Obs.                     270          230

=====================================================================
                   Point    Robust Inference
                Estimate         z     P>|z|      [ 95% C.I. ]       
---------------------------------------------------------------------
     RD Effect     0.099     7.077     0.000     [0.070 , 0.123]     
=====================================================================
```


:::
:::


## Reading the `rdrobust` Output {.smaller}

Focus on these five numbers from the summary above:

::: incremental
-   **BW est. (h):** The data-driven **optimal bandwidth** (in test-score points). `rdrobust` picks it for you — no more guessing
-   **Eff. Number of Obs.:** How many observations actually contributed to the estimate on each side — narrow bandwidths shrink the effective sample
-   **Coef. (Conventional):** The **point estimate** of the jump at the cutoff — this is your treatment effect
-   **\[ 95% C.I. \] (Robust):** The bias-corrected confidence interval — **report this one** in a paper
-   **P\>\|z\|:** The p-value — is the jump statistically distinguishable from zero?
:::

. . .

::: callout-tip
## Report Both Intervals

Show both the **Conventional** and **Robust** CIs. If they tell the same story, the estimate is stable. If they diverge noticeably, your bandwidth is driving the result and you need to investigate.
:::

## The `rdplot` Visualization {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# rdrobust comes with a companion plotting function
rdplot(y = df$completion, x = df$score, c = cutoff,
       title = "RD Plot with Automatic Binning",
       x.label = "Test Score",
       y.label = "College Completion Rate")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


`rdplot` bins the data and shows the local polynomial fit — a publication-ready RDD figure.

## Should You Add Controls? {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   The short answer: **probably not for identification, maybe for precision**

-   The logic: if the cutoff creates as-good-as-random assignment, there's no omitted variable bias to fix

-   But adding covariates that predict the outcome can **reduce noise** and tighten confidence intervals

-   Think of it like a randomized experiment — you don't *need* controls for identification, but they can help with precision

-   **Which covariates?** Use variables measured *before* the cutoff that predict the outcome

    -   *Safe and helpful:* family income, demographics, prior GPA, baseline test scores, lagged outcomes
    -   *Dangerous:* anything measured *after* treatment — it may itself be affected by the cutoff, which biases your estimate
:::

. . .

::: callout-note
## In English

Controls in RDD aren't about fixing confounding (the cutoff handles that). They're about statistical power — predicting more of the outcome variance so the treatment effect estimate is more precise.
:::

## Kernels: Weighting by Distance {.smaller}

An alternative to sharp bandwidth cutoffs: **weight observations** by how close they are to the cutoff.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
h <- 10   # bandwidth = 10 test-score points
x_kern <- seq(-h, h, length.out = 500)
w <- pmax(1 - abs(x_kern) / h, 0)

ggplot(tibble(x = x_kern, weight = w), aes(x = x, y = weight)) +
  geom_area(fill = "grey80") +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-h, h), linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Triangular Kernel Weight Function",
       subtitle = "Bandwidth h = 10 test-score points around the cutoff",
       x = "Distance from Cutoff (test-score points)", y = "Weight") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


Observations closer to the cutoff get **more weight**. This is what `rdrobust` does under the hood.

# Part 4: Policy Examples

## Example 1: The Welsh Shingles Policy {.smaller}

**The policy.** In September 2013, the Welsh NHS introduced a shingles vaccination program for older adults. The eligibility rule was a **sharp birth-date cutoff**: adults born on or after **September 2, 1933** were eligible; adults born before that date were deemed too old per the guidance.

**Why this matters for dementia research:**

::: incremental
-   Shingles is caused by reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus — the same virus that causes chickenpox in childhood
-   Emerging hypothesis: chronic viral infections may contribute to dementia risk
-   Hard to test causally — people who get vaccinated differ from those who don't (wealthier, more health-conscious, better access to care)
-   The Welsh birth-date cutoff removes that confounding entirely. **You cannot manipulate your birth date.**
:::

**The RDD setup:**

-   **Running variable:** Week of birth relative to Sept 2, 1933
-   **Cutoff:** Week 0 (Sept 2, 1933)
-   **Treatment:** Eligibility for the shingles vaccine
-   **Main outcome:** New dementia diagnosis within 7 years

*Source: Eyting et al. (2025), Nature*

## First Stage: Did the Cutoff Change Vaccination? {.smaller}


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


**What you're seeing:** each dot is a birth-week cohort. X-axis is week of birth relative to the Sept 2, 1933 cutoff. Y-axis is the percent who eventually received the shingles vaccine.

**The jump at week 0 is \~47 percentage points** — from about 30% to about 77%. A 4-week age difference at the cutoff produced an enormous difference in treatment. This is a very strong first stage.

## Placebo Checks: Did Anything Else Change? {.smaller}


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


**The worry:** what if people born just before vs. just after the cutoff differ in some *other* way — not just eligibility? Then we'd confound the vaccine effect with something else.

**The check:** look for jumps in *pre-treatment* outcomes — things measured BEFORE the 2013 policy started.

::: incremental
-   **Panel (b):** Prior shingles diagnoses. Flat across the cutoff (coef ≈ 0.1). Groups had similar shingles histories.
-   **Panel (c):** Prior dementia diagnoses. Smooth downward trend (older people → more dementia), but **no jump** at the cutoff. Groups were comparable.
:::

**The takeaway:** the cutoff changed vaccination rates (panel a) and *nothing else* (panels b, c). That's the RDD ideal — identification comes from the policy, not from underlying differences.

## The Punchline: Vaccines Reduce Dementia {.smaller}


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


When the jump is this clear, you almost don't need statistics. That's the beauty of a good RDD.

## Example 2: Medicare at Age 65 {.smaller}

Card, Dobkin, and Maestas (2008) used the 65th-birthday cutoff to study the effect of near-universal health insurance.


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Reading the Medicare Panels {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   **Panel (a) — First stage:** Coverage jumps from \~82% to \~97% at the 65th birthday. Eligibility is sharp, and take-up is nearly universal
-   **Panel (b) — Outcome:** Doctor visits per year rise from \~4 to \~5.2 at 65 — newly-insured people use more healthcare
-   **Why this is credible:** you cannot manipulate your age, and nothing else changes abruptly at exactly 65. The cutoff is truly arbitrary
-   **LATE interpretation:** effect applies to people *right at* 65. We cannot learn the effect of Medicare on 40-year-olds from this design
:::

. . .

::: callout-tip
## Bridge Back to the Opener

We started today with **Medicaid expansion** — a DiD design exploiting *state-level* variation in policy. Now we're looking at **Medicare at 65** — an RDD design exploiting *age-level* variation in eligibility. Same substantive question ("does insurance improve health?"), two different identification strategies. Having both in your toolkit is the point.
:::

## More RDD Examples in Policy {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   **Voting age and political participation:** Turning 18 creates a sharp cutoff for eligibility — used to study whether early voting experience shapes lifelong habits

-   **Drinking age and mortality:** The legal drinking age at 21 creates a sharp jump in alcohol consumption and, tragically, in traffic deaths (Carpenter & Dobkin, 2009)

-   **Title I funding:** Schools above a poverty threshold receive federal funding — used to study whether additional resources improve outcomes

-   **Close elections:** When one candidate barely wins vs. barely loses, outcomes are as-if randomly assigned — a popular design in political science (Lee, 2008)
:::

. . .

::: callout-tip
## The Pattern

Notice the pattern across these examples: an **administrative rule** creates a cutoff → people just above and below are essentially the same → comparing their outcomes reveals the causal effect. Look for these rules in your policy area!
:::

# Part 5: Sharp vs. Fuzzy RDD

## Sharp RDD: Perfect Compliance {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(scales)

# Local cutoff just for these illustrative diagrams
c_demo <- 0.5
x_grid <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 500)

df_sharp <- tibble(x = x_grid, p = ifelse(x_grid < c_demo, 0, 1))

ggplot(df_sharp, aes(x = x, y = p)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.5, color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c_demo, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1L),
                     breaks = seq(0, 1, by = 0.25)) +
  labs(title = "(a) Sharp Design: Treatment Probability",
       x = "Running Variable", y = "Proportion Treated") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


In a **sharp** design, the cutoff perfectly determines treatment: 0% below, 100% above.

## Fuzzy RDD: Imperfect Compliance {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
df_fuzzy <- tibble(
  x = x_grid,
  p = ifelse(x_grid < c_demo, 0.15 + 0.2 * x_grid, 0.55 + 0.2 * x_grid)
)

ggplot(df_fuzzy, aes(x = x, y = p)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.5, color = "black") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c_demo, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1L),
                     breaks = seq(0, 1, by = 0.25)) +
  labs(title = "(b) Fuzzy Design: Treatment Probability",
       x = "Running Variable", y = "Proportion Treated") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-18-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


In a **fuzzy** design, the cutoff causes a **jump** in the probability of treatment — but it's not 0-to-100%.

## Fuzzy RDD in the Wild: Maimonides' Rule {.smaller}

Angrist and Lavy (1999): Israeli schools follow a rule attributed to Maimonides — **no more than 40 students per class**. A school with 41 students *should* split into two classes of \~20.


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


**Schools don't perfectly comply.** Angrist and Lavy use *only the variation in class size that the rule predicts* — setting aside school-level choices — to isolate a causal effect. Their key finding: smaller classes improve test scores, especially for disadvantaged students. We'll walk through how this actually works in a moment.

## How Fuzzy RDD Actually Works {.smaller}

When the rule *shifts* treatment probability but doesn't perfectly determine it, fuzzy RDD combines **two jumps** at the cutoff into one effect estimate.

::: incremental
1.  **Measure the jump in treatment at the cutoff.** For Maimonides: at enrollment = 41, actual class size drops by about 8 students on average — *not* the full 20 the rule predicts, because compliance is imperfect

2.  **Measure the jump in the outcome at the cutoff.** Say test scores rise by 0.2 standard deviations across the same threshold

3.  **Scale.** *If* we believe the cutoff affects outcomes **only through** class size, each 1-student change in class size explains 0.2 / 8 ≈ **0.025 SD** of test-score improvement
:::

$$\hat{\tau}_{fuzzy} = \frac{\text{jump in outcome at cutoff}}{\text{jump in treatment probability at cutoff}}$$

## What We Just Did: Instrumental Variables {.smaller}

This is **instrumental variables (IV)** in action — a causal-inference technique for when treatment isn't randomly assigned. The "instrument" here is the cutoff itself: it shifts treatment but, we hope, doesn't otherwise affect the outcome.

**IV in general needs three things:**

::: incremental
1.  The instrument actually **changes treatment** — we showed this with the jump in class size
2.  The instrument is **as good as random** with respect to the outcome — true at the cutoff, where a 4-week age or 1-student enrollment difference can't plausibly sort people on unobservables
3.  The instrument affects the outcome **only through treatment** — the strong assumption we unpack next
:::

**Why fuzzy RDD is a relatively well-behaved form of IV:**

::: incremental
-   The first stage is **visible in a plot** — you can literally see whether the cutoff moves treatment
-   Assignment at the cutoff is nearly random *by construction*
-   "Only through" is easier to argue about a narrow threshold than for an instrument coming from somewhere else in the world
:::

*We won't cover standalone IV in this course — it's a whole separate methodology, and usually more controversial than its fuzzy-RDD cousin.*

## The Catch: Why Fuzzy Estimates Deserve Scrutiny {.smaller}

Fuzzy RDD requires a strong assumption — and it mechanically amplifies what it reports.

::: incremental
-   **The "only through" part is heavy.** For Maimonides, is class size really the *only* thing that changes at enrollment = 41? What about teacher assignments, classroom resources, peer composition, or parents sorting across schools? If any of these also moves the outcome, the scaling gives you the wrong answer — and the assumption is rarely directly testable

-   **Scaling amplifies noise.** Dividing a small, imprecise outcome jump by a small, imprecise treatment jump produces a bigger estimate *and* a wider confidence interval. A modest reduced-form result can become a dramatic-sounding fuzzy estimate
:::

. . .

::: callout-important
## Bottom Line

A sharp RDD result is usually easier to defend than a fuzzy one. When you encounter a fuzzy RDD in a paper or report, ask: *what else might be changing at that cutoff?* and *how strong is the first stage?* Be more skeptical than you would be with a sharp design.
:::

## Fuzzy RDD in `rdrobust` {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Fuzzy RDD: pass the actual treatment indicator to `fuzzy`.
# rdrobust handles the two-jump scaling for you — but the scaling
# is only as honest as the "only through" assumption on the prior slide.
rd_fuzzy <- rdrobust(y = outcome, x = running_var, c = cutoff,
                     fuzzy = treatment_indicator)
summary(rd_fuzzy)
```
:::


. . .

::: callout-tip
## Sharp or Fuzzy?

If the cutoff **perfectly determines** treatment → Sharp RDD. If the cutoff **shifts the probability** of treatment → Fuzzy RDD. When in doubt, plot the treatment probability against the running variable. If it's a step function, it's sharp.
:::

# Part 6: Validity Checks

## Manipulation Testing: The McCrary Density Test {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   If people can **manipulate** the running variable, we'd see **bunching** just above (or below) the cutoff

-   The McCrary (2008) density test checks: is the distribution of the running variable smooth through the cutoff?

-   A "pile-up" on one side suggests manipulation — and invalidates the design
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
set.seed(30317)

# Build a side-by-side comparison: clean vs. manipulated scores.
n_bunch       <- 80   # ~16% of students "cheated" into the 80-82 range
bunched_score <- c(
  runif(500 - n_bunch, 60, 100),  # baseline uniform
  runif(n_bunch,        80, 82)   # concentrated spike just above cutoff
)

df_density <- bind_rows(
  tibble(score = df$score,      condition = "Clean: no manipulation"),
  tibble(score = bunched_score, condition = "Manipulated: bunching above cutoff")
)

ggplot(df_density, aes(x = score)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "gray70", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cutoff, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ condition) +
  labs(title = "What Manipulation Looks Like",
       x = "Test Score", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-21-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


**Left:** smooth density through the cutoff — what we want. **Right:** a clear pile-up just above 80 — a red flag that students may have manipulated their scores to qualify.

## Formal Manipulation Test {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# The rddensity package provides a formal test
library(rddensity)

density_test <- rddensity(X = df$score, c = cutoff)
summary(density_test)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

Manipulation testing using local polynomial density estimation.

Number of obs =       500
Model =               unrestricted
Kernel =              triangular
BW method =           estimated
VCE method =          jackknife

c = 80                Left of c           Right of c          
Number of obs         270                 230                 
Eff. Number of obs    94                  86                  
Order est. (p)        2                   2                   
Order bias (q)        3                   3                   
BW est. (h)           6.418               6.44                

Method                T                   P > |T|             
Robust                0.2058              0.837               


P-values of binomial tests (H0: p=0.5).

Window Length              <c     >=c    P>|T|
1.228     + 1.228          20      20    1.0000
1.804     + 1.807          25      28    0.7838
2.381     + 2.386          30      33    0.8013
2.958     + 2.965          38      38    1.0000
3.534     + 3.544          45      45    1.0000
4.111     + 4.123          55      57    0.9248
4.688     + 4.703          69      69    1.0000
5.264     + 5.282          76      74    0.9350
5.841     + 5.861          88      82    0.7015
6.418     + 6.440          94      86    0.6020
```


:::
:::


. . .

A non-significant p-value means we **fail to reject** the null of no manipulation — which is what we want. If significant, the RDD may be compromised.

## Placebo Tests {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   **Placebo cutoffs:** Run the RDD at *fake* cutoff values where there's no treatment. If you find "effects," something is wrong.

-   **Covariate balance:** Check whether *pre-determined characteristics* (demographics, prior outcomes) show a jump at the cutoff. They shouldn't.

-   **Donut hole test:** Remove observations *very* close to the cutoff and re-estimate. If the result changes dramatically, those boundary observations may be problematic.
:::

## Placebo Cutoffs in Action {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Run rdrobust at real and fake cutoffs; only the real one should show an effect
placebo_cutoffs <- c(70, 75, 80, 85, 90)   # 80 is the real cutoff

placebo_results <- map_dfr(placebo_cutoffs, function(c_val) {
  rd <- rdrobust(y = df$completion, x = df$score, c = c_val)
  tibble(cutoff   = c_val,
         is_real  = c_val == 80,
         estimate = rd$coef[1],
         ci_lower = rd$ci[1, 1],
         ci_upper = rd$ci[1, 2])
})

ggplot(placebo_results, aes(x = cutoff, y = estimate, color = is_real)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper), width = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "#e31a1c", `FALSE` = "gray40"),
                     labels = c(`TRUE` = "Real cutoff", `FALSE` = "Placebo")) +
  labs(title = "RDD Estimates at Real vs. Placebo Cutoffs",
       x = "Cutoff Value (Test Score)",
       y = "RDD Estimate (completion rate)", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-23-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


Only the cutoff at 80 should show a real effect. Placebo cutoffs should be indistinguishable from zero — that's what we see.

## The Donut Hole Test {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
donut_values <- c(0, 0.5, 1, 2)   # donut radius in test-score points

donut_results <- map_dfr(donut_values, function(d) {
  keep <- abs(df$score - cutoff) >= d
  rd <- rdrobust(y = df$completion[keep], x = df$score[keep], c = cutoff)
  tibble(donut    = d,
         estimate = rd$coef[1],
         ci_lower = rd$ci[1, 1],
         ci_upper = rd$ci[1, 2])
})

ggplot(donut_results, aes(x = donut, y = estimate)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper), width = 0.08) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  labs(title = "Donut Hole Test: Stability When Near-Cutoff Points Are Excluded",
       x = "Donut Radius (test-score points excluded on each side)",
       y = "RDD Estimate (completion rate)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-24-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


If the estimate is **stable** as we exclude observations closer to the cutoff, that's reassuring. Wild swings would suggest those boundary observations are driving — or contaminating — the result.

## Covariate Balance Check {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Simulate a pre-determined covariate (family income, in $000s)
# — should NOT jump at the cutoff if the RDD is credible
set.seed(30317)
df$family_income <- 30 + 0.4 * df$score + rnorm(nrow(df), 0, 5)

ggplot(df, aes(x = score, y = family_income, color = group)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cutoff, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Covariate Balance: Family Income by Test Score",
       subtitle = "No jump at the cutoff = good news for the RDD",
       x = "Test Score", y = "Family Income ($000s)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("No Scholarship" = "#1f78b4",
                                "Scholarship"    = "#e31a1c")) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-25-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


Covariates should pass smoothly through the cutoff. A jump here would be a red flag.

## Sensitivity to Bandwidth {.smaller}


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Check if the estimate is stable across bandwidth choices
library(rdrobust)

bandwidths <- c(2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15)   # in test-score points

sensitivity <- map_dfr(bandwidths, function(h) {
  rd <- rdrobust(y = df$completion, x = df$score, c = cutoff, h = h)
  tibble(bandwidth = h,
         estimate  = rd$coef[1],
         ci_lower  = rd$ci[1, 1],
         ci_upper  = rd$ci[1, 2])
})

ggplot(sensitivity, aes(x = bandwidth, y = estimate)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lower, ymax = ci_upper), width = 0.3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  labs(title = "Sensitivity to Bandwidth Choice",
       x = "Bandwidth (test-score points)",
       y = "RD Estimate (completion rate)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture14_RDD_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-26-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


If the estimate is **stable** across bandwidths, that's reassuring. Wild swings suggest fragility.

# Part 7: Practical Advice

## Checklist + Common Pitfalls {.smaller}

**Before you trust an RDD — yours or someone else's:**

::: incremental
1.  Running variable is **precisely measured** and hard to manipulate
2.  **Density** is smooth through the cutoff (no bunching)
3.  **Covariates balance** at the cutoff
4.  Estimate is **robust** to different bandwidths
5.  **Placebo cutoffs** show null effects
6.  If **fuzzy**, first stage is strong
:::

. . .

**Pitfalls to avoid:**

::: incremental
-   Too-wide bandwidth — people far from the cutoff aren't comparable
-   High-order polynomials — can manufacture fake jumps (Gelman & Imbens 2019)
-   Forgetting **LATE** — your estimate applies at the cutoff, not everywhere
-   Not showing the plot — RDD is visual; the picture *is* the evidence
-   Assuming sharp when it's fuzzy — biases the estimate toward zero
:::

## Readings {.smaller}

-   **Huntington-Klein, *The Effect*, Ch. 20** — intuitive treatment with great examples (free online)
-   **Cunningham, *Causal Inference: The Mixtape*, Ch. 6** — detailed chapter with R code (free online)
-   **Cattaneo, Idrobo, and Titiunik (2020)** — *A Practical Introduction to Regression Discontinuity Designs*. The definitive applied guide
-   **Lee and Lemieux (2010)** — "Regression Discontinuity Designs in Economics." The classic review

# Wrapping Up

## What We Covered Today {.smaller}

::: incremental
1.  **One last DiD walkthrough** — Medicaid expansion and mortality
2.  **The RDD idea** — cutoffs as local randomization
3.  **The graphical recipe** — plot, fit, bandwidth, estimate the jump
4.  **Estimation** — from OLS to `rdrobust` with optimal bandwidth selection
5.  **Policy examples** — shingles/dementia, Medicare at 65
6.  **Sharp vs. fuzzy** — with Maimonides' Rule as the fuzzy example
7.  **Validity checks** — manipulation, placebo cutoffs, donut holes, bandwidth sensitivity
:::

# Doing Quant Work as an MPA

## Four Habits That Separate Good Analysis from Statistical Theater {.smaller}

::: incremental
1.  **Start with the question, not the method.** A simple t-test on the right comparison beats a fancy model on the wrong one. Design follows the question — not the other way around

2.  **You'll mostly be a consumer, not a producer.** Most of your career you'll read, commission, and push back on analyses. Learn to ask: *"What's the comparison? How do you know they're comparable? Who does this estimate apply to?"*

3.  **Show the picture.** Every method we covered has a visual signature — parallel pre-trends for DiD, a jump at the cutoff for RDD, balanced baselines for experiments. If the plot doesn't tell the story, be suspicious of the coefficient that does

4.  **Be honest about the limits.** Every estimate has assumptions. Every result applies to a specific group. Saying "we can't tell from this data" is often more useful — and more credible — than overselling a small effect
:::

## What This Course Was Really About {.smaller}

::: callout-tip
## The Real Lesson

We spent the semester on methods — OLS, fixed effects, DiD, RDD, experiments. The real lesson is **mental discipline**: being precise about *what question you're answering*, *what assumptions you're making*, and *what your result does and doesn't mean*.

You don't need to be the person who runs every analysis. You do need to be the person who knows **what to ask, what to trust, and what to reject**. That's how quantitative work actually shapes policy — and it's what makes you valuable.
:::

## For Your Capstone {.smaller}

::: callout-tip
## Think About This Now

Does your policy area have **eligibility thresholds**, **age cutoffs**, **geographic boundaries**, or **test score requirements**? If so, you may have a natural RDD waiting to be discovered. The key ingredients: a running variable you can measure, a cutoff that changes treatment status, and an argument that people just above and below are comparable.

Even if you don't run the analysis yourself, knowing how to *read* RDD (and DiD, and IV, and experiments) critically is a career-long skill.
:::

<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
